7:7 seems to imply that Paul has the gift of self control. So both verses seems to be asking the saints to follow Paul in this example. So the sense of verse 8 and 9 is this: "I say to the unmarried and widows to discipline themselves as I do. But if they cannot contain themselves, let them marry. It is "better to marry than be consumed with passion."
It appears Paul is teaching to go counter to the Corithinan norms - the unmarried and widows, if not careful, could be caught up in the immoral practices of the time.

Chastity and purity are things of the greatest importance to the world. Hence the Prophet says, "Because the Lord hath been witness between thee and the wife of thy youth, against whom thou hast dealt treacherously; yet is she thy companion, and the wife of thy covenant. And did not he make one? Yet had he the residue of the Spirit. And wherefore one? that he might seek a godly seed. Therefore…

Why be concerned with the passing preferences of the world anyway? “For the fashion of this world passeth away” (1 Corinthians 7:31). (Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Apr. 1987, 86; or Ensign, May 1987, 70)

But if the private members in the Christian Church were permitted to have more than one wife, why not also the bishops and deacons ? Paul has not given us the reason. It is quite probable, that the principal reason was that the important duties devolving upon these offices required them to be as free from other cares as possible.
